Collection › Song Dynasty › ro-song-015 — Guan Yao Chrysanthemum-Lobed Bowl

Reference Object ro-song-015

Guan Yao Chrysanthemum-Lobed Bowl with Tianqing Glaze

官窑 菊瓣碗 — Southern Song Dynasty, 1127–1279 CE — Laohudong, Hangzhou

Southern Song Guan Ware Tianqing Glaze 天青釉 Chrysanthemum Form Jinsi Tiexian Kou Tie Dronova Method DEE · SDE · LIE · PLIC

Object Description

A chrysanthemum-lobed bowl with a gently curved profile, multi-lobed rim following the form of chrysanthemum petals, and a narrow ring foot. The thick calcium-alkali glaze of Tianqing tone (天青, sky-blue) covers all surfaces with pronounced optical depth and a characteristic luminous jade-like quality. The lobed rim carries a naturally formed Kou Tie (口鐵) darkening where the glaze thins at the petal ridges, and the ring foot displays the Tie Zu (鐵足) effect with the dark iron-rich stoneware body exposed.

The Jinsi Tiexian (金絲鐵線, Golden Thread and Iron Wire) double crackle system is fully developed: wide primary cracks with dark iron-rich mineral infill constitute the Tiexian network, while finer secondary cracks with lighter golden coloration form the Jinsi system within the primary polygons. The crackle propagates continuously across interior, exterior, and base surfaces.

Microscopic Diagnostic Analysis — Dronova Method

This bowl was examined using Gemmological Optical Microscopy (GOM) applying the Dronova Method — a systematic four-category optical authentication protocol for Southern Song Guan ware. Unlike methods requiring SEM, XRF, or Raman spectroscopy, the Dronova Method documents all principal diagnostic microstructures directly through the intact glaze surface without sampling.

The Dronova Method evaluates four complementary diagnostic categories simultaneously. No individual category alone is sufficient for authentication; rather, the internal consistency of all four optical systems forms the diagnostic basis. This object illustrates the complete application of the method and serves as a primary reference specimen for the Atlas.

DEE

Dry Earth Effect — Natural Ageing

Naturally mineralised crackle channels with long-term soil infiltration. Glaze surface weathering. Oxidation of the iron-rich foot. Differential ageing between glazed and unglazed surfaces consistent with centuries of natural burial and environmental exposure.

SDE

Structural Decorative Effect — Manufacturing Technology

Organically asymmetric chrysanthemum lobes. Glaze pooling between petals. Naturally formed Kou Tie at thinned rim. Authentic glaze draw-bead above the foot. Characteristics of hand manufacture rather than modern mechanical production.

LIE

Luminescence–Interference Effect — Optical Depth

Internal jade-like luminosity from multiple light-scattering mechanisms. Depth-dependent optical scattering producing thickness-dependent colour variation. Bubble-rich glaze. Optical evidence of nanoscale phase separation in the calcium-alkali glass matrix.

PLIC

Polychrome Layer and Inclusion Complex — Crackle System

Authentic Jinsi Tiexian double crackle: wide iron-filled primary Tiexian cracks and fine secondary golden Jinsi cracks. Three-dimensional crack depth. Micro-pits related to ancient glaze alteration. Optical morphology expected for Southern Song Guan ware.

GOM Documentation — DEE, PLIC, LIE

DEE / PLIC

Fig. 5. PLIC — Double crackle detail (Jinsi Tiexian). Primary Tiexian crack (wide, dark mineral infill) intersected by finer secondary Jinsi cracks (lighter, golden coloration). Three-dimensional depth differentiation confirms sequential thermal crack formation rather than mechanical fracture.

DEE

Fig. 6. DEE — Microstructure of the ceramic body beneath the glaze layer. Compacted mineral inclusions and vitrified particles characteristic of high-temperature reduction firing. The granular body fabric and oxidation pattern are consistent with centuries of natural ageing of an iron-rich stoneware body.

GOM Documentation — LIE: Glaze–Body Interface

LIE

Fig. 7. LIE — Footrim and glaze junction at high magnification. The glaze matrix displays the bubble population and internal light-scattering microstructure responsible for the jade-like Tianqing luminosity. The dark iron-rich body is sharply bounded by the calcium-alkali glaze layer. The glaze–body interface shows natural diffusion rather than mechanical application.

1. DEE — Dry Earth Effect

Long-term natural ageing is documented in mineralised crackle channels with gradual mineral infill consistent with centuries of burial, differential ageing between glazed and unglazed surfaces reflecting different weathering environments, and oxidation of the exposed iron-rich foot ring. The DEE category evaluates whether the observed ageing pattern is internally consistent across the entire object — which it is here, supporting a unified long-term environmental history.

2. SDE — Structural Decorative Effect

The chrysanthemum-lobed form exhibits organically asymmetric petal geometry without mechanical regularity, glaze pooling in petal valleys reflecting viscous glaze flow during firing, a naturally formed Kou Tie band where the glaze thins at petal ridges, and an authentic glaze draw-bead above the foot. These structural features could only form through original hand-throwing and hand-lobing combined with authentic glaze application and firing.

3. LIE — Luminescence–Interference Effect

The glaze exhibits the characteristic internal jade-like luminosity of authentic Southern Song Guan ware, produced by depth-dependent optical scattering from the bubble-rich glaze microstructure and nanoscale liquid–liquid phase separation within the calcium-alkali glass matrix. The LIE is not a surface property but an internal optical system: thickness-dependent colour variation (the pale blue-grey of the thin rim glaze versus the deeper grey-green of the thicker base glaze) directly reflects the multi-layer depth of the internal scattering population.

4. PLIC — Polychrome Layer and Inclusion Complex

The Jinsi Tiexian double crackle system presents the full expected optical morphology: wide primary Tiexian cracks with dark iron-rich mineral infill forming the Iron Wire network, and finer secondary Jinsi cracks with lighter golden coloration forming the Golden Thread system within the primary polygons. Three-dimensional depth differentiation between the two crack generations confirms sequential thermal formation. Micro-pits related to ancient glaze alteration are present in the crackle zone, consistent with DEE-category ageing.

Diagnostic Interpretation

The combined evidence from all four Dronova Method categories — DEE, SDE, LIE, PLIC — provides a comprehensive and internally consistent optical authentication profile. No individual feature alone constitutes proof; it is the agreement of all four diagnostic systems that forms the basis for the high authentication confidence assigned to this object.

Diagnostic SystemPrincipal Features Observed
DEE — Dry Earth EffectMineralised crackle channels, differential ageing, iron foot oxidation, consistent long-term environmental history
SDE — Structural Decorative EffectAsymmetric lobed form, glaze pooling in petal valleys, Kou Tie, glaze draw-bead — all consistent with hand manufacture
LIE — Luminescence–Interference EffectInternal jade-like luminosity, thickness-dependent colour variation, bubble-rich glaze, nanoscale phase separation
PLIC — Polychrome Layer and Inclusion ComplexJinsi Tiexian double crackle with sequential depth differentiation, mineral infill, ancient micro-pits

Key Diagnostic Markers

DEE: Mineralised crackle channels — centuries of natural ageing confirmed
DEE: Differential ageing between glazed and unglazed surfaces
SDE: Organically asymmetric chrysanthemum lobed form
SDE: Glaze pooling in petal valleys — original firing behaviour
SDE: Kou Tie (口鐵) naturally formed at thinned petal rim
LIE: Tianqing jade-like internal luminosity (天青)
LIE: Thickness-dependent colour gradient (rim vs centre)
LIE: Bubble-rich glaze and nanoscale phase separation
PLIC: Jinsi Tiexian double crackle (金絲鐵線) — full morphology
PLIC: Sequential depth differentiation of two crack generations
Tie Zu (鐵足) — dark iron-rich stoneware foot ring
High internal consistency across all four diagnostic categories

Diagnostic Confidence: Very High — Catalogue No. GY-CHR-12-S-01
